How Habits of Mind Are Turning Art Students into Science Enthusiasts 






























					Science teacher Lissette Rodriguez uses a weekly sticky note activity where students reflect on how their peers helped them succeed.				




 Image generated using MidJourney AI










In a school where creativity and artistic expression take center stage, science often doesn’t top the list of favorite subjects. At Miami Arts Studio in Miami, Florida, teacher Lissette Rodriguez is flipping that narrative.By leveraging the Habits of Mind, she’s cultivating a positive attitude toward science in her students, leading them to embrace the subject—not out of obligation, but as a natural result of a transformative learning process.Lissette teaches biology, anatomy, and AP Biology to students who are primarily focused on the arts. Despite this, she has seen many of her students develop a genuine passion for science and even pursue science-related careers after graduation.“I teach in an art school, so science is not the strong suit of my students,” said Lissette. “But I try to make it the strongest one. By the end, they love science and go into a lot of science careers, just because of that positive mindset.” 




 








Encouraging Reflection with Habits of Mind 



At the core of her teaching is Metacognition — Thinking About Your Thinking, which encourages students to reflect on their thoughts, strategies, and feelings, as well as how their actions impact others.Lissette integrates this habit into her classroom through regular reflection activities that help students develop self-awareness and improve their approach to learning.“Every Friday, we reflect: How did we do this week? What worked? How can we make it better? How can we fix it?” said Lissette.This consistent focus on reflection helps students become more intentional in their learning, creating an environment where they feel empowered to tackle challenges and celebrate progress. 







Cultivating Curiosity, Confidence, and Persistence 



“When you walk into this room, you need to be open,” said Lissette. “We’re going to discuss everything, ask questions, and develop a discussion.”This open and exploratory approach aligns seamlessly with the Habits of Mind, fostering a classroom culture that prioritizes curiosity and adaptability. Students are encouraged to persist even when faced with difficulties, and they quickly realize that their struggles are part of the learning process.“We didn’t get it the first time,” said Lissette. “But how can we actually get it? What do we need to change?”Persisting is also central to Lissette’s teaching philosophy. She encourages her students to analyze their challenges, reflect on their strategies, and try again. This process helps them develop the grit and resilience necessary to succeed—not just in science, but in life. 







A Natural Love for Science 



The result is transformative. Lissette’s students aren’t just learning science—they’re learning how to engage with the world in a way that opens doors to new possibilities. By reflecting on their thinking and embracing a positive attitude, Lissette’s students are discovering a love for science that feels natural and unforced. 



She uses this classroom strategy as a tool: 



Lissette uses a simple but powerful activity to foster positivity and collaboration in her classroom:Set the Scene: Divide students into small groups of 4–5.Weekly Reflection: At the end of each week, ask students to write a sticky note highlighting something positive a group member did to help them succeed.Share the Notes: Encourage students to share their notes with their group. This reinforces teamwork and helps students feel recognized and valued.Impact: “By the end, they love the class and come back for more classes, even hard ones,” said Lissette. “They see that they can do it, and they are open to it.”Through her work, Lissette demonstrates how the Habits of Mind can extend beyond academic success to shape students’ perspectives and aspirations.In her art-focused school, science has become not just a subject but a gateway to a new way of thinking and engaging with the world. 







Develop Lifelong Skills At The Habits of Mind Conference 



Educators like Lissette exemplify the power of small, intentional actions to spark major transformation. At the upcoming Habits of Mind Conference on April 7-8 in Greece, New York, educators and leaders will come together to explore similar stories of success and learn practical strategies to create lasting change.With a focus on Radical Optimism, the conference offers an opportunity to gain actionable insights and connect with a global community of educators transforming the future, one habit at a time. Register now!

Inside the IPP Cohort Experience: How a Small Learning Community Sparks Global Impact 






























					At the Institute for Habits of Mind, the Individual Practitioner Program (IPP) is intentionally designed to foster deep learning, real connection, and personal transformation.				




 





At the Institute for Habits of Mind, the Individual Practitioner Program (IPP) is intentionally designed to foster deep learning, real connection, and personal transformation. Centered around small cohorts, the experience brings together tight-knit groups of educators and leaders who journey together through collaborative reflection, practice, and growth. While professional development can often feel impersonal or one-size-fits-all, the IPP Program offers something entirely different: a transformative, intimate learning journey that creates lasting global connections.
Each cohort begins with more than just introductions. Participants are invited to bring their own contexts, questions, and challenges into the space. Through weekly live sessions, shared readings, and facilitated reflection, members begin to learn from one another as much as from the curriculum. Many alumni describe the cohort as “the most human PD I’ve ever done” and “a space where I finally felt seen as both a teacher and a learner.”
Past cohorts have included educators from across the U.S., as well as participants from international schools and organizations in Europe, Asia, and South America. These diverse perspectives enrich the experience, offering new ways to interpret, adapt, and apply the Habits in varied cultural and institutional settings.
The program culminates in a Celebration of Learning, where participants present their projects and reflect on their personal journeys. For many, it’s a deeply affirming moment—proof that small, intentional communities can spark real change on a global scale.
The next IPP cohort begins July 28, and spaces are limited. If you’re an educator, coach, or organizational leader looking to deepen your practice, build meaningful professional relationships, and become part of a growing global network, this is the program for you. The Habits of Mind aren’t just ideas. They’re actions, decisions, and ways of being, shared and amplified through community. Join us, and help expand this impact, one thoughtful habit at a time.
Click here to register

Embracing Radical Optimism: 2025 Habits of Mind Conference Theme 































 










We have been inspired by a podcast interview with Fredrik Pferdt and Dan Harris and were so enthusiastic we read his book What’s Next is Now. Pferdt talks about radical optimism —a way to develop a future ready mind-set as we navigate ambiguity and uncertainty with thoughtful intention. The ideas in this book stimulated our connections to the Habits of Mind. In thinking about “what’s next is now”, we decided to build the conference around who we are as individuals and who we are collectively as members of the Habits of Mind community. 



Whether a participant is new to Habits of Mind or is an avid believer in the framework, the wide range of sessions will explore how to engage with students, families, colleagues to co-create our future. From the classroom to the system level, we hope to provide opportunities to network with a passionate and highly skilled group of professionals, to inspire and plan for action. 










						Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!					

						April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY					


						Learn More!					











DAY 1: Monday, April 7 



Building the Future: Empowering Students with Radical Optimism & Habits of Mind 



Today’s sessions explore how to cultivate the Habits of Mind in students, empowering them to become active agents in their own learning. We awaken a canvas of possibilities with our students by equipping them with tools and strategies for developing the Habits of Mind.Throughout the day, you’ll gain valuable skills and exchange ideas in these areas:Identifying powerful classroom strategies: discover engaging activities and real-world examples to help students develop critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity.Creating a growth mindset: Explore strategies to cultivate a positive learning environment where students embrace challenges and view setbacks as opportunities for growth.Empowering student agency: Learn how to shift the focus from teacher-centered to student-centered learning, giving students ownership over their learning journey. 







DAY 2: Tuesday, April 8 



Leading From Within: Cultivating Your  Inner Strength with Habits of Mind, Movement, and Heart 



This day is a journey of self-discovery for leaders at all levels, where we assume leadership is described by a set of behaviors regardless of position. We’ll explore how the Habits of Mind can nourish your inner strength, allowing you to lead with greater authenticity and impact.Throughout the day, you’ll gain valuable skills and exchange ideas in these areas:Prioritizing Self-Care: Discover strategies for nurturing your well-being and creating sustainable leadership practices.Cultivating a Culture of Growth: Foster a learning environment where everyone thrives in the school community.Embracing Innovation: Lead courage and adaptability, navigating the evolving educational landscape.Don’t miss this unique opportunity to experience the power of the Habits of Mind firsthand!Connect with a vibrant community of passionate educators and thought leaders who share your dedication to the Habits of Mind. Discover new strategies for implementing the Habits of Mind in your learning organization and leave feeling inspired and empowered.Register now!

Cultivating Thoughtful Leadership: Using Habits of Mind with Your Board of Education 































 










Boards of Education play a critical role in shaping the system’s future. Their decisions affect not only the financial stability of the organization but also its mission, values, and culture. With this immense responsibility, board members must bring more than just strategic thinking to the table — they must embody a set of thoughtful practices that ensure leading with empathy, interdependence, and innovation. The Habits of Mind, a set of 16 problem-solving skills developed by Arthur Costa and Bena Kallick, can be instrumental in guiding board members through the complex challenges of school governance. 



Below are three essential Habits of Mind that, when put to use in the work of the Boards, can elevate and strengthen communication and problem solving: 




Listening with Understanding and Empathy
Thinking Interdependently
Questioning and Posing Problems

These habits align closely with the National Association of Independent Schools (NAIS) Principles of Good Practice for Independent School Trustees and the National Association of State Boards of Education (NASBE), which emphasize the importance of collaboration, long-term planning, and speaking as one voice.								










						Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!					

						April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY					


						Learn More!					







Listening with Understanding and Empathy 



A foundational principle of effective board leadership is creating space for others to share their thoughts. In a boardroom, where diverse opinions and perspectives are essential to making informed decisions, board members must practice the habit of Listening with Understanding and Empathy. This habit asks board members to engage with one another and avoid responding immediately or defending a position. Rather, they listen attentively and genuinely work to understand where others are coming from.For example, a board member may have strong feelings about a particular budget item, but listening to a colleague’s concerns about the long-term impact on faculty salaries or student financial aid can open the door to a more comprehensive discussion. By practicing active listening, board members not only enhance their own decision-making but also foster an environment where all voices are valued. Listening builds trust and ensures that decisions are made with a full understanding of their implications. 




 




In addition to listening, empathy is a critical habit for board members. School boards often have to balance the needs of various stakeholders, including students, parents, faculty, and the broader community. However, it’s important to remember that the Superintendent or Head of School is the board’s only employee. As such, board members need to understand the immense responsibility the superintendent carries and be empathetic to the pressures that come with leading a district.When board members approach decisions with empathy—whether it’s about tuition rates, programmatic changes, or even school policies—they are better able to align with the school’s core values. Empathy allows board members to consider the perspectives of all those affected by their decisions while maintaining a clear focus on the school’s long-term goals. This balance of empathy and strategic thinking is what enables board members to make compassionate yet mission-driven decisions. 



Thinking Interdependently 



Boards are most effective when they function as cohesive units, with each board member understanding how their actions impact the larger system. The habit of thinking interdependently aligns closely with systems thinking, which recognizes that decisions in one area of the school have ripple effects on other interconnected areas.For example, in a private school setting a decision to increase tuition will not only influence enrollment and financial aid but could also affect the overall diversity and accessibility of the student body. Board members must approach decision-making holistically, analyzing these interdependencies to ensure they are considering the schools as a dynamic, interconnected ecosystem, rather than each operating in silos. 




 




In many ways, board members are like a finely tuned orchestra: each member plays a distinct part, but together they must harmonize to create a cohesive sound. Individual passion is important, but board members must always keep in mind how their contributions support the collective good. This is especially true in moments of tension or disagreement, where thinking interdependently allows the board to move from individual positions to collaborative solutions that strengthen the entire organization.Principles of Good Practice for school board members emphasize the importance of collaboration, strategic planning, and unity in decision-making. One of the key principles is that board members accept and support the final decisions they make, speaking with one voice. When board members think interdependently, they recognize that even if they initially disagree with a decision, they must support it as part of the broader strategic goals of the school. 



Questioning and Posing Problems 



The boardroom should be a place where innovation and strategic foresight are encouraged. The habit of questioning and posing problems invites board members to move beyond the immediate challenges and ask, “What if?” or “How might we?” By using tentative language, these questions push the board to explore potential challenges and opportunities, fostering a culture of inquiry that prevents stagnation. 




 




For instance, when considering long-term financial sustainability, board members should ask not only how to meet current needs but also how emerging trends—like changes in educational technology or shifts in demographics—might impact the school’s future. By posing these forward-thinking questions, board members help their schools stay ahead of challenges and remain adaptable in an ever-changing landscape.The principle that board members are responsible for setting policy and engaging in long-term planning reflects the need for Questioning and Posing Problems. By continuously asking critical questions, board members help ensure that they remain forward-looking and aligned with the system’s mission. 



Final Reflection 



Incorporating the Habits of Mind into board governance can transform how board members approach their responsibilities, fostering a more collaborative, empathetic, and innovative boardroom culture. When board members listen for understanding, empathize with stakeholders, think interdependently, and question assumptions and consider possibilities, they not only enhance their own leadership but also contribute to the long-term success of the system. As schools continue to evolve, so too must the practices of those entrusted with their stewardship. The Habits of Mind provide a valuable framework for doing just that. 



About Regina McDuffie 



Dr. Regina McDuffie is the Head of School at The Pegasus School in Huntington Beach, Calif. She focuses on future thinking and innovation that supports the mission of the school, using the Habits of Mind to guide her leadership. She is also a Certified Individual Practitioner.

How Can You Create a Purpose-Driven Classroom with the Habits of Mind? 






























					"When students face a challenge, instead of seeing it as a failure, we frame it as an opportunity to apply persistence, flexibility, and problem-solving." -- Dan Vollrath				




 Photo created by Midjourney










As educators navigate the challenges of modern classrooms, fostering a learning environment where students develop resilience, self-awareness, and purpose has never been more critical.
Dan Vollrath, a special education teacher and author of The Purpose-Driven Classroom, has dedicated his career to integrating Habits of Mind into daily learning, helping students build not only academic skills but also the mental and emotional strength necessary for lifelong success.
“I always tell myself, if I can start the day with something hard, the rest of the day is going to be easy,” said Vollrath. “That mindset helps me stay focused and energized, and it’s the same principle I try to instill in my students.”
At the upcoming Habits of Mind Conference (April 7-8, Rochester, NY), Vollrath will lead sessions on student autonomy, emotional intelligence, and building a purpose-driven learning environment. His approach is centered on small, intentional habits that make a significant impact over time. 







Bringing Habits of Mind into the Classroom 



For Vollrath, the Habits of Mind serve as the foundation for his teaching philosophy.“It’s not just a lesson—it’s a mindset. We journal about habits, we discuss them in class, and we reflect on them daily,” he explained. “I want students to recognize how these habits show up in their lives, not just in school but in the decisions they make outside of it.”In his classroom, students regularly reflect on questions like, What Habit of Mind helped you succeed this week? or Which habit do you need to strengthen? This practice encourages metacognition, helping students recognize their growth and take ownership of their learning. 




 








Helping Students Build Resilience 



Vollrath emphasizes that resilience is a learned skill, and the way educators design their classrooms plays a crucial role in developing it.
“When students face a challenge, instead of seeing it as a failure, we frame it as an opportunity to apply persistence, flexibility, and problem-solving,” he said. “That’s how we help them build resilience—not by avoiding struggles but by equipping them with the tools to navigate them.”
His upcoming session, The Purpose-Driven Classroom: Seven Productive Behaviors for Student Success, will explore strategies to help educators embed resilience-building practices into their curriculum. 







The Power of Community and Collaboration 



Beyond his classroom, Vollrath is passionate about fostering a community of educators who support each other in bringing Habits of Mind to life.
“We learn so much from each other. Some of the best strategies I use in my classroom came from conversations with other teachers,” he shared. “At the conference, I’m looking forward to those discussions—the ones that happen not just in sessions but in hallways, over lunch, and after presentations. That’s where the magic happens.” 



Join the Conversation 



Vollrath invites educators to join him at the Habits of Mind Conference to explore how intentional habits shape successful students.
“This work isn’t just about improving test scores—it’s about shaping students into critical thinkers, problem-solvers, and lifelong learners,” he said. “If that’s the kind of impact you want to have, I’d love to see you there.”

From the Inside Out: How Habits of Mind Transformed One Elementary Educator’s Teaching Practice 






























					For Janice Mix, teaching health at the elementary level isn’t just about sharing facts, it’s about helping young learners build the skills they’ll need to thrive in and beyond the classroom. 				




 





For Janice Mix, teaching health at the elementary level isn’t just about sharing facts, it’s about helping young learners build the skills they’ll need to thrive in and beyond the classroom. A graduate of the Institute for Habits of Mind’s Individual Practitioner Certification (IPP) program, Janice recently shared her story on the What’s the Big Idea? podcast, highlighting how Habits of Mind reshaped both her professional lens and her students’ capacity for self-awareness, regulation, and growth.
In her role as a full-time elementary health educator, Janice saw firsthand the need for frameworks that support emotional regulation, decision-making, and resilience — especially at a young age. Habits of Mind gave her just that: a way to make abstract concepts like persistence, flexibility, and metacognition tangible for students as young as five.
What sets Janice’s story apart is not just how she implemented the Habits, but how her experience in the IPP cohortfueled her personal transformation. The IPP is an online program designed for educators, leaders, and coaches to deepen their understanding and use of Habits of Mind in meaningful, context-driven ways. Unlike generic professional development, these cohorts are small, interactive, and reflective. Participants engage in real dialogue, apply what they’re learning in real time, and, importantly, equip participants to become more intentional leaders not just in instruction, but in culture-building.
The takeaway? Habits of Mind aren’t just something you teach, they’re something you practice. And with programs like the IPP, the Institute can help educators do both with clarity and confidence.
Want to be part of the next wave of reflective educators? Subscribe to the IHOM newsletter for updates on upcoming IPP cohorts, new tools, and growth opportunities.
Listen to Janice’s full interview on What’s the Big Idea?: Habits of Mind at the Elementary School Level

The Habits of Mind Advantage: Why These Skills Make You More Employable in a Rapidly Changing World 






























					Employers across industries are seeking individuals who can think critically, collaborate effectively, adapt gracefully, and continuously learn. These are the Habits of Mind.				




 













 



In an era defined by uncertainty, rapid innovation, and evolving workplace demands, traditional technical skills are no longer enough. Employers across industries are seeking individuals who can think critically, collaborate effectively, adapt gracefully, and continuously learn. These are the Habits of Mind.
Originally developed by Dr. Arthur Costa and Dr. Bena Kallick, the 16 Habits of Mind are a set of dispositions that support thoughtful, strategic, and reflective action. While deeply rooted in educational settings, these habits are now gaining traction in corporate and organizational leadership circles for a simple reason: they work.
 
Why Habits of Mind Matter in the Workplace
The modern workplace demands more than task completion. It asks employees to manage ambiguity, solve complex problems, and communicate with empathy. Habits like Thinking Flexibly, Questioning and Posing Problems, Listening with Understanding and Empathy, and Managing Impulsivity directly support this kind of agile, effective work.
A recent article in StrategicCHRO360 highlights the shift from learning a skill to fostering lifelong learning: “HR leaders need to stop training employees and start teaching them how to learn,” Katie Kuehner-Hebertexplained. This is precisely where Habits of Mind excel. They offer a language and structure for cultivating metacognitive awareness and social-emotional competence—core pillars of professional growth and leadership readiness.
 
Leadership That Thinks Differently
Dana Lesperance, a long-time IHOM practitioner, offers a powerful perspective: effective leaders embody these habits. From Persisting through challenges to Applying Past Knowledge to New Situations, these are not abstract ideals—they are observable behaviors that make leaders more resilient, thoughtful, and human-centered.
Lesperance emphasizes the habit of Remaining Open to Continuous Learning as central to 21st-century leadership. As organizations evolve, the most valuable professionals will be those who can admit gaps in their knowledge and seek growth. In short: to be employable is to be a learner.
 
A Growing Demand Beyond Education
As Habits of Mind continue to gain recognition outside of schools—in HR departments, professional learning communities, and even corporate development spaces—the Institute for Habits of Mind is responding.
Launching soon: a new website experience featuring AI-enhanced tools to support reflection, lesson planning, and problem-solving through the lens of these 16 Habits. This expansion is centralized around helping people lead with intention, collaborate with curiosity, and grow with purpose.
Want early access to the new tools and training programs?Join the IHOM mailing list and be the first to explore new offerings, including AI-based tools and upcoming leadership workshops.

Radical Optimism at the Habits of Mind Conference: Shaping the Future Through Action 






























					Embracing Uncertainty to Shape a Better Tomorrow				




 










Every challenge presents an opportunity. That’s the core of Radical Optimism, the theme of the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference. This powerful concept, inspired by Dr. Fredrik Pferdt, invites us to reframe uncertainty as a chance for growth, learning, and transformation. 







The Path to Radical Optimism 



The idea for this year’s theme began with a simple yet profound moment. While listening to a podcast featuring Dr. Pferdt, conference co-director Allison Zmuda was struck by his insights into shaping the future with intention. She immediately ordered his book, What’s Next is Now, and shared its concepts with Bena Kallick, co-director of the Habits of Mind Institute. Together, they recognized a deep connection between Radical Optimism and the Habits of Mind, leading them to weave it into the heart of this year’s conference. 



What Does Radical Optimism Mean? 



Radical Optimism isn’t about ignoring challenges — it’s about embracing them as opportunities to create something new. Dr. Pferdt challenges us to shift from asking ‘What will the future bring?’ to ‘What future do I want to create?’ This small but profound change puts us in the driver’s seat of our own learning and development.The Habits of Mind — such as Thinking Flexibly, Persisting, Managing Impulsivity, and Remaining Open to Continuous Learning — are essential tools for cultivating this mindset. When applied to education, Radical Optimism empowers students, teachers, and leaders to co-create the future rather than passively accept it. 



Conference Highlights 



The 2025 Habits of Mind Conference will feature engaging sessions that explore:Practical strategies to build a future-ready mindsetHow educators can create environments that foster curiosity and adaptabilityWays to strengthen psychological safety in classrooms and organizationsThe role of experimentation in shaping learning and leadership 



Engaging Interview with Dr. Fredrik Pferdt 



To deepen the conversation, Dr. Pferdt joined Allison and Bena for an exclusive interview, sharing powerful insights on Radical Optimism, adaptability, and the habits that help us navigate an uncertain world. 




 








A Call to Action 



The future isn’t something that happens to us — it’s something we create. By embracing Radical Optimism, we move beyond fear and into action, shaping what’s next with intention and creativity.Join us for this inspiring event where we take the next step toward a future defined by curiosity, resilience, and learning.🚀 Don’t miss your chance to experience Radical Optimism!📅 Habits of Mind Conference – April 7-8, Rochester, NY🔗 Register now!

Applying the Habits of Mind to Hiring and Decision-Making 






























					How Habits of Mind Help Leaders Think Critically, Adapt, and Make Better Decisions				




 










What if the key to hiring the right leaders and decision-makers wasn’t just in their resumes, but in the way they think?In today’s unpredictable and fast-moving world, organizations need more than just qualified candidates — they need adaptive thinkers, problem solvers, and reflective decision-makers. The Habits of Mind provides a powerful lens for evaluating not only students and educators, but also leaders, boards, and hiring committees.During a recent discussion previewing the Habits of Mind Conference, taking place April 7-8 in Rochester, NY, leaders explored how these habits can be integrated into the hiring process and decision-making at all levels of an organization. 







Transforming Hiring Practices 



Regina McDuffie, Ph.D., head of a preschool-through-eighth-grade school in Southern California, shared how she began applying Habits of Mind to leadership and governance. As she prepared for upcoming interviews, she realized the framework could offer a more structured way to assess candidates.“As I prepare to interview for some leadership positions here, I’ve been thinking about the interview questions,” McDuffie said. “I’m thinking about the Habits of Mind, but I never thought about how to get the team that is working on that interview committee to use it in that way of assessing and evaluating in a more structured way.”Margie Sills-Maerov, a healthcare professional specializing in coaching and team development, reinforced this perspective by sharing how she integrated Habits of Mind into the hiring process for a hospital CEO.“If somebody walked through my door and had all these skills, I’d hire them in a heartbeat,” Sills-Maerov said. “The wonderful thing is, everybody does have these skills. The question is, how do you start to elevate that and create conditions for people?” 




 








Sills-Maerov developed an interview guide that measured a candidate’s ability by looking for the following Habits of Mind:Thinking Flexibly: Being able to change perspectives; generating alternatives, considering options.Questioning and Posing Problems: Having a questioning attitude; knowing what data are needed and developing questioning strategies to produce those data. Finding problems to solve.Striving for Accuracy: Doing your best. Setting high standards. Fact checking and finding ways to improve.Thinking About Your Thinking (Metacognition): Being aware of your own thoughts, strategies, feelings and actions and their effects on others.“We overlaid [the hiring process] with Habits of Mind and coaching to find a new CEO for an organization that is on the precipice of huge change,” Sills-Maerov said. “By assessing the habits, you were able to assess what kind of thinker this person is and whether they could be adaptive.” 







Reflection in Leadership Decision-Making 



Beyond hiring, educational consultant Michelle Hughes highlighted the importance of applying Habits of Mind at every level of leadership, ensuring decisions are made with deliberation, adaptability, and collective problem-solving.“The things that Regina shared and the things that Margie shared in terms of how Habits of Mind function in these very divergent ways at every level of an organization really resonated with me,” Hughes said. “You begin with yourself — yourself as a leader, yourself as a teacher, yourself as a consultant — and how you reflect and grow your own habits of mind.”She emphasized that self-awareness among leaders is crucial for fostering a culture where employees feel encouraged to think critically and creatively.“It’s so authentic in that way, and that’s the trajectory I always bring to my own courses,” Hughes said. “It’s so important.” 



A Culture of Inquiry and Continuous Growth 



Integrating Habits of Mind into hiring and leadership isn’t just about identifying strong candidates — it’s about cultivating a culture that encourages ongoing learning and adaptability.“The accessibility and flexibility of Habits of Mind allows organizations to rethink how they mentor, evaluate, and support employees,” Sills-Maerov said. “If you think about hiring as not just filling a position, but rather building a thinking culture, the approach completely shifts.”McDuffie agreed, adding that embedding the framework at all levels of governance strengthens leadership alignment.“Sometimes supporting trustees means helping them persist, but also knowing when to pause and reflect,” McDuffie said. “It involves flexible thinking and knowing how to question and pose problems effectively.”As organizations continue to prioritize resilience, adaptability, and strategic decision-making, the Habits of Mind framework offers a scalable, research-backed approach that extends far beyond the classroom.Whether used in hiring, performance evaluation, or board leadership, these habits create a culture of inquiry, reflection, and continuous improvement, one decision at a time. 







About the Habits of Mind Conference 



To explore these insights further and connect with thought leaders who are shaping the future of education and leadership, attend the Habits of Mind Conference, April 7-8 in Rochester, NY.This event will offer keynote presentations, hands-on workshops, and collaborative discussions to help educators, administrators, and professionals apply these powerful habits in their own organizations. Register today to be part of the movement towards more thoughtful, adaptive leadership.

“Failure is Not Always My Fault”: A Transformative Perspective from Dana Lesperance 































 










At the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference, attendees will hear a message that transcends education, touching the core of personal growth and resilience. Dana Lesperance, head of the Community High School of Vermont, will present a session titled Rebuilding Trust with Our Students Through Habits of Mind, where he challenges traditional notions of failure.
His approach redefines how individuals perceive setbacks — not just as educators or leaders, but as human beings navigating life’s challenges.
“Not to sound disrespectful to students, but (it’s true that) ‘Failure is not always my fault,'” said Dana when speaking to how he often leads with Taking Responsible Risks when engaging students around the Habits of Mind.
“That’s what our students have experienced so much: failure. But through reflection on Habits of Mind, they learn to say, ‘Not yet. I’ll get there.’” 




 








Habits That Transform Mindsets 



Key to Dana’s philosophy is the idea that Habits of Mind are more than 16 strategies for success—they are a transformative framework for approaching life. Students are encouraged to persist, strive for accuracy, and think flexibly. These habits become a “backpack” of tools they carry with them into every challenge, empowering them to tackle obstacles with confidence.
“When our students can say, ‘I’m using my habits,’ we know we’ve succeeded in building a foundation of trust,” said Dana. “Once you’ve established that culture, you’ve built trust—they want to show up for you. They want to show up for themselves.” 







Why This Message Matters 



Dana’s message reaches far beyond the walls of his school. It challenges everyone—educators, administrators, parents, and professionals—to rethink their relationship with failure. Habits of Mind offer a pathway to personal resilience, teaching individuals to reflect, adapt, and grow. These lessons are invaluable in today’s fast-changing world, where setbacks are inevitable, but resilience is a choice.
“Habits of Mind are what tie together the student experience,” said Dana. “They allow us to move students forward, no matter their starting point.” 







The Takeaway for Attendees 



Attendees of Dana’s session will walk away with practical strategies for:

Using reflection to reframe failure as an opportunity for growth.
Building resilience through Habits of Mind, both personally and professionally.
Creating environments where individuals feel empowered to take responsible risks and persist.

This session is not just for educators. Anyone seeking tools to foster resilience and personal growth will find Dana’s insights invaluable. 







Don't Miss Out! 



Last year’s Habits of Mind Conference left attendees inspired to make meaningful changes in their personal and professional lives. This year promises to take those insights even deeper. Dana’s session is one you can’t afford to miss.
Register now to secure your spot at the conference in Greece, New York, this April. Early Bird rates are ending soon — don’t wait to be part of this transformative experience.

Bringing Habits to Life: How Animated Stories Help Young Learners Practice Thinking Flexibly, Persisting, and More 






























					Young learners thrive on visuals, repetition, and storytelling. That’s why the Habits of Mind Kids animated video series is quickly becoming a go-to tool for educators and families aiming to introduce powerful thinking habits early. 				




 





Young learners thrive on visuals, repetition, and storytelling. That’s why the Habits of Mind Kids animated video series is quickly becoming a go-to tool for educators and families aiming to introduce powerful thinking habits early. Developed in partnership with the Institute for Habits of Mind, the series translates abstract concepts, like Managing Impulsivity or Listening with Understanding and Empathy, into stories that children can see, feel, and practice.
Each short video features engaging characters who face real-world dilemmas and learn to navigate them using one of the 16 Habits of Mind. The animations are designed to spark conversation and offer entry points for SEL-based reflection across grade levels and contexts.
“Children love anything that makes them feel like briefcase-carrying grown-ups,” says Dr. Bena Kallick, co-creator of the Habits of Mind framework. “Teaching what persisting looks like, sounds like, and what it is to practice ‘persistence’ in one’s approach to new challenges—these are crucial elements when instilling habits into young minds.”

Why This Works for Early Learners
The Habits of Mind Kids series is grounded in evidence-based strategies for social-emotional learning and metacognition. It blends storytelling with SEL best practices by:

Making habits concrete and memorable through animated characters
Supporting self-regulation and empathy in real-time scenarios
Reinforcing shared language across home and school environments

The short length of the videos, generally under three minutes, makes them easy to integrate into morning meetings, brain breaks, or guided conversations. They’re also ideal for modeling classroom norms in younger grades or prompting group reflection in school counseling sessions.

A Scalable Resource for Classrooms and Families
The videos are housed on HabitsOfMindKids.com, where educators and caregivers can access a growing library of animations aligned with each Habit. Designed for PreK–5 audiences, the site is educator-informed, and includes both free and subscription-based content. Whether you’re introducing Thinking Flexibly for the first time or reinforcing Applying Past Knowledge to New Situations, there’s a clip ready to support your lesson.

Building the Habit Early
By introducing these thinking dispositions through stories and shared reflection, students learn how to pause, plan, collaborate, and make thoughtful decisions, even in the earliest grades. These are the seeds of lifelong learning, and they grow best when nurtured with intention.
Explore the full series and bring Habits of Mind to life in your classroom or home: www.habitsofmindkids.com

Leadership, Joy, and Radical Optimism: Joelle Weaver Returns to the Habits of Mind Conference 






























					Practical wisdom from the front lines of educational leadership.				




 










Few voices speak about Habits of Mind with as much heart, experience, and impact as Joelle “Joey” Weaver. As principal of an intermediate school in the Honeoye Falls-Lima Central School District in New York, Joey has not only led systemic integration of Habits of Mind across K–12, she’s also been a passionate conference participant, contributor, and advocate.This year, she returns to the Habits of Mind Conference as a featured presenter, co-leading two dynamic sessions with Adele Pecora:Leadership Perspectives on Cultivating Habits of MindFinding Joy in the Journey: Sustaining the Work of Habits of Mind 






Registration Extended to March 29!











But before taking the stage, Joey was once a first-time attendee … an experience that deeply affirmed her commitment to the work.“One of the most powerful things we saw immediately was the sense of community amongst people who had never met each other,” said Joey about last year’s conference. “Everyone around you is looking at the world through those same 16 dispositions. It’s really hard to make tangible the amount of energy, kindness, love, compassion, connection that you find at a Habits of Mind conference.” 



 



At last year’s event, Joey brought two teachers (seen above): one a long-time leader in the initiative, the other brand new.The shared takeaway? The unifying power of common language across vastly different experiences and backgrounds.“Our students sort of live and breathe the Habits,” Joey shared. “I even have grandparents in our community commenting on Habits that their grandchildren who attend my school talk about.” 



This year's theme, Radical Optimism, resonates deeply with Joey. 



“Optimism is hope, and hope is what makes all of the Habits of Mind function,” said Joey. “When you’re joyous about what you’re doing, it’s really hard to stop momentum. You get a lot of people on board.”For Joey, this kind of joy is both a mindset and a leadership approach. Her presentation, Finding Joy in the Journey, is grounded in sustaining meaningful work over time and helping educators tap into the inner joy that drives lasting change.And when asked what Habit of Mind most connects to Radical Optimism? 





				“You have to be flexible. You have to constantly be malleable. And optimism ... I think finding joy is where that's at. So I would say if you're Thinking Flexibly and you're looking for joy, you're radically optimistic.”			





Whether you’re new to Habits of Mind or decades into the work, Joey’s voice is one to learn from — filled with warmth, wisdom, and an unwavering belief in the transformative power of Habits-based education.Conference Registration Extended — Now Closing Friday, March 29!Don’t miss your chance to attend the Habits of Mind Conference in Greece, NY (April 7–9). Join educators and leaders like Joey Weaver for sessions that will refresh your spirit and sharpen your practice.
